item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, this one proved a little tricky for me. Talking with Fantem as to possible connections, SCP-142 came up. This are two very different articles, and connecting them directly may be contrived at best, however, if they were found in the same place that might raise a few questions. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Changing this:

SCP-1825 was found after an incident on 01/██/20██, where an abandoned arcade game factory in ███████ collapsed. One body was found in the area, ███████ ████, a vagrant presumably seeking shelter inside the factory. It is assumed that ███████ ████ activated SCP-1825, and died from the debris created from leveling the building. SCP-1825 was found in the wreckage, protected from the debris by several steel girders.

To this:

SCP-1825 was found after an incident on 01/██/20██, where an abandoned arcade game factory in ████████ collapsed. One body was found in the area, ███████ ████, a vagrant presumably seeking shelter inside the factory. It is assumed that ███████ ████ activated SCP-1825, and died from the debris created from leveling the building. SCP-1825 was found in the wreckage, protected from the debris by several steel girders. Due to the proximity of recovery to SCP-142, investigation into the potential for a shared origin between the two objects is currently ongoing.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me laugh so hard!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just want to say that I absolutely love this, and find it to be criminally underrated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got it. Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When the subject pulls SCP-1825's, the hatch will release one metal ball

Something's missing here….

Don't know what to call it. Handle?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This thing is delightful. Sure, it leads to ultraviolence, but it's almost cartoony in a way.

Of course it would never kill the bugger, but does anyone else think that getting a good pinball player to use this on 682 would be one of the funniest damn things to watch ever?
(Note, not advocating a cross test. Just… picture it in your mind, and smile.)

item.title: fixing things
item.content:encoded: Well, I made some general fixes to this. Basically removed the word 'proxy' because I thought that sounded awkward, and other things. Input is welcomed by all. Ɛ:

item.title: Re: Well...
item.content:encoded: Well, safety classes aren't really a rating of how 'safe' something is; it's a rating of how safe it is to keep it behind bars. Regardless of how deadly this is, it comes with an ON/OFF switch. You could make an argument that this is actually 'safer' than SCP-999.

item.title: Well...
item.content:encoded: I would not call this a Safe. Not dangerous enough to be Keter, but hell, it has commited multiple acts of murder (Or at least the proxy did)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, OK…I see what you mean. Never mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not necessarily. I was reading it as the electronic score display/flashing lights/advertising back board was what stood that high, while the actual play field was at standard height (~4 feet? I dunno, it's been ages since I played pinball)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, the pinball machine is 2m in height? That's about 6 and 1/2 feet; you'd have to stand on a ladder (or be 9 feet tall) in order to play it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm still quite a newbie around here, so take this FWIW, but I'm afraid this doesn't really work for me. The writing style seems too casual in some places, and in my opinion, the image doesn't enhance the article (plus the caption is worded strangely—does the aftermath need to be contained?). Just doesn't float my boat. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm planing on doing a re-write on this when I finish my current project. I'll probably trim the logs a bit. Or something. Should be able to get to work on it soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really love the idea— and imagery— but the presentation lessened the impact. Maybe the test logs were a few too many? Doesn't read especially funny or scary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this dang thing, but the implied "For Pete's sake it could turn the entire freakin' planet into a pinball" sealed it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No problem!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Allright then. So, the proxy is the room according to the room in the pinball machine, not the ball. Thanks for explanation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, the different rooms are meant to test the shape (or distortion) of the proxy. The spherical room just kind of bit them in the ass during testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, that is it. I can't really imagine what is going on there: The walls are lined with motion sensors to detect dents or possible damage in the big ball?
And why are such different rooms used? I guess it has something to do with the forbidden use of the circular room, but I can't quite put my finger on it. Then again, it might just be that I'm not an english speaker and some words don't quite click there for me as I imagine what's going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please take out all those footnotes. There's no need to define the common features of a well-known device.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! But what do you mean about the Environment test logs? I'm just trying it out in different rooms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what is going on in the enviromental log. Otherwise, nice idea!

item.title: Re: Very, very interesting
item.content:encoded:
Actually, I did bring that up in test number 9, although it was D-class personnel. I'd imagine it having the same problems with SCPs (potentially catastrophic events could ensue. I'd imagine the situation like trying to dig a hole with a nuclear bomb) although, it might be interesting to make a supplement entry based on that premise.
Glad you liked it!

item.title: Very, very interesting
item.content:encoded: Thruout the read, I kept thinking, 'How useful will this be in containment of the Euclid and maybe even Keter Class SCPs? I know cross SCP testing is forbidden, but what abt other circumstances?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See? that's why I'm torn between keeping it the way it is or not. On one hand, you could say it's too long, but on the other, you could say it doesn't feel long because it builds suspense. Might need to find a happy medium. Thanks for the for the feedback! Never had someone say they loved my work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright

  • I'll change that once I finish this.
  • Well, I was thinking they built a bunch of rooms after they got it. no way they could have all of those rooms (which are steel plated) on hand. As for the triangular room… I dunno, I guess I could say "3-walled" or something.
  • oh, test 12 answers that.
  • It's just the proxy that has the weight, not the pinball. the pinball's just a pinball… kinda.
  • basically, everything is frozen in place until 1825 is off. Then, flop, crash, or shatter respectively.
  • will do.

Thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understood it right off the bat what it did, how the foundation acquired it, and why it may be useful to keep around! I love this SCP. I didn't feel anything wrong with it whilst I read, good show. The logs didn't drag on for me, in-fact their slowly building effects only made me wonder just how far they'd go to test it's properties.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote for now. I have a few gripes though.

  • Density is measured in kg/m^3, not kg/m^2.
  • I know the Foundation is big, but the thought that there are just random, pre-existing cylindrical, triangular (which is two dimensions, is it a prism? A tetrahedron?), and spherical rooms…it breaks suspension of disbelief for me.
  • These situational tests don't many any sense. Test 11, the density of the proxy keeps on increasing, but you don't say how the test was resolved.
  • A standard sized pinball has a volume of 10.32 cm^3. With your "high score" over 30 million, your pinball weighs 312,940kg, almost 700,000 pounds. (A maxed out pinball would weight 1.0320e+7 kg, over 22 million pounds) And the D-Class just picked it up and put it in the drain? Explain what's going on here.
  • I can't follow "When the ball goes into the drain, everything that collided with the proxy ceases to be supported by SCP-1825, and collapses." Collapses, like falls down? The language for support and collapse here isn't clear to me.
  • "[SCP-1825] is never to be activated outside or inside a spherical room <see addendum-1825-01-B>." Use a comma between outside and or, use parentheses as you have in the rest of the article instead of angle brackets, and capitalize Addendum.

I like a tiny pinball of death but I have a lot of question marks around how this thing actually works.

item.title: Re: OW.
item.content:encoded: Instead of making three consecutive posts in two minutes, use the "Edit" button under "Options" to add commentary to your existing post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thank you! yeah, that was a complaint in the beta stages, but honestly, I don't know what I would take out. I was thinking maybe test numbers 1 and 5, but I'm hesitant.
It's like I'm a hoarder, but instead of objects filling up my house, it's text filling up my entries.
edit: same goes for mere sentences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't saying to cut them…just streamline a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the logs should stay, actually. Limits the machine and provides interesting insight as to its functions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is highly amusing, and it made me cringe with horror when I read it. Only suggestion I have is maybe reducing the logs a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enjoyable and clever enough to warrant an upvote. A nice, simple safe-class object.

item.title: Re: OW.
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed!

item.title: Re: OW.
item.content:encoded:
"Objects unaffected from the proxy are SCP-1825, SCP-1825-1, and any objects and persons entering the room after activation. "

Had a dumb. Carry on!

item.title: Re: OW.
item.content:encoded: Also I gave this a +, after fixing one spelling mistake.

item.title: OW.
item.content:encoded: Dangerous in all the right ways! Although how does the machine keep from damaging itself?
